chunk_summary: Guidance on hydration counseling for children, including daily water intake recommendations, monitoring hydration status, and appropriate beverage choices based on age and activity levels.
learning_objectives: Understand daily water intake recommendations for children across different age groups.
Learn appropriate beverage choices for children, including water, milk, and traditional fluids.
Recognize signs of dehydration and implement monitoring cues for hydration status.
Identify hydration strategies for specific conditions like hot weather or physical activity.
key_facts: Daily water intake (AI) for children: 1.3 L/day (1–3 years), 1.7 L/day (4–8 years), 2.1 L/day (girls 9–13 years), 2.4 L/day (boys 9–13 years), 2.3 L/day (girls 14–18 years), 3.3 L/day (boys 14–18 years).
Foods contribute roughly one-third of total water intake, so beverage volumes are lower than AI targets.
For infants 0–6 months, breastmilk/formula meets hydration needs; extra water is not advised.
Signs of adequate hydration: regular daytime voiding and pale-yellow urine; dehydration signs include dark urine, lethargy, and reduced urination.
Avoid sugar-sweetened beverages and energy drinks for routine hydration; water should be the default choice.
reference_quote: Recommended drinks for young children ages 0–5: 'Water and plain milk are the best choices for young children.' (AAP HealthyChildren.org)
document_key_topics: Daily water intake recommendations for children
